I drive a 2018 Prius Prime and live in Kansas. It has an 8.8kWh battery. I've only ever charged at home. It costs $0.06/kWh to charge for a total of $0.528 per full charge. I get about 23 miles of range per charge. That comes out to $0.0229 per mile. I drive less than 20 miles per day, so I pretty much only use gas when going out of town. I routinely drive more than 1,000 miles between fill-ups, and it only takes 9.8 gallons of gas. I've driven about 13,000 miles in the last 15 months and average about $30 per month in gas and about $16 per month in electric charges. Combined, that's about $0.053 per mile. At my last oil change, they asked if I really wanted one because it didn't seem to need it. Regenerative braking saves the brake pads, and the car is light and slow, so there's little wear on the tires. Insurance is $120 per month. Annual registration is about $400. Priuses are extremely reliable. I've owned the car for 15 months and have had zero unscheduled maintenance costs. The car was about $20,000 after I received a $4,000 tax credit and it had 36,000 miles on it when I bought it. It is paid off. I owned a 2008 Prius before that and sold it for $4,000 with 201,000 miles on it. It took one day to sell. Including electric, gas, insurance, registration, and two annual oil changes at $100 each, my ownership cost on a monthly basis is about $215. I say all this just to illustrate that EVs don't have to be a luxury purchase, and if you can combine an economical car with cheap electric rates and a lifestyle that fits the car's EV-range limitations, it can be a great financial decision. Of course, I'm still in the red after only 15 months of ownership, as I had to buy the car, but I intend to keep the car for another 100,000 miles (or about 9.5 more years, at the rate I'm driving). Over time, I'm confident I'll save a lot. As a point of illustration, I really want a 2026 Honda Passport. But I ran the numbers and found that even if I were to sell my Prius for $20,000 and put that down on a $50,000 Passport, the approximate additional ownership costs (considering all the factors I talked about above plus interest on a car loan) are about $60,000 more than the Prius after 100,000 miles of driving. I'm way beyond my original point by now, but I can't justify an extra $500 per month for 10 years, regardless of how much more practical the Passport is. $50,000, by the way, is the average price of a new car today. Now imagine you're rolling negative equity into a purchase like that and/or have bad credit. It's cars, not just gas, that are too expensive!

I keep hearing how the rising fuel costs could cause inflation to rise. Which makes absolutely no sense to me yet not once, not anywhere has a detailed explanation been offered for how this correlation makes any logical sense. 

I get that higher fuel prices are a cause for goods and services across the board to also rise. But beside from a retail point of view where the anticipated average sales for a week/month/quarter may be regarded as likely to increase due to higher unit price for the expected sales. And with that perspective extrapolated across the sector it can be taken from this frame of reference that the money supply in circulation is forecast to expand. 
BUT
With households largly having depleted their savings pool (and the main transaction of private equity now being the liquidation of the family home in a major city and relocating to regional centres to purchase a smaller/cheaper house/villa for retirement.) and the weekly budget already having to find expendable costs in order to reallocate disposable incomes for any additional expenditures, the simple fact of the matter is that there's nearly no margin left for the money in circulation -which can be attributed to private household spending- to grow and any growth experienced in any one sector, especially critical sectors -like energy, rent/mortgage, food or tax for eg- is alkost exclusively going to have to come at the cost of negative growth in one or more other sectors.

In other words, a family only has so much weekly income, which in 3/4 of cases is almost completely consumed by essential costs and only a small fraction if any, remains for entertainment or personal expenditure. If there's a fixed supply and any one of a group of costs increase, servicing that cost comes at the expense of a reduced allocation of the budget for spending on or buying other things. 
The cost of fuel can go up 1,000,000% it doesn't mean the people are like bankers and can just conjure up extra money at will.

Logistics businesses — particularly fuel delivery — are designed to operate at peak efficiency based on normal weekly and monthly demand volumes. Maintaining surplus drivers and tankers beyond what's typically needed isn't an oversight; it's a deliberate cost decision. Excess capacity sitting idle is a direct drain on operating margins.
When demand spikes abnormally, that lean model hits its limits fast. Unlike ride-sharing, you can't simply onboard casual drivers to operate specialised, heavily regulated tanker vehicles. These machines require specific licensing, training, and compliance — there's no "surge driver" pool to tap into.
Cost pressures compound the problem. Carriers and operators use fuel themselves, and when prices rise sharply, they cannot simply absorb that additional overhead. Contract renegotiations become inevitable — and that takes time.
The broader point is this: consumers have grown accustomed to frictionless, next-day delivery across many industries. Fuel supply chains don't work that way. They are precision-calibrated systems, not elastic ones. When the inputs change suddenly — whether that's demand, price, or workforce availability — the whole chain feels it.
Expecting fuel delivery to behave like an Amazon parcel is a fundamental misunderstanding of how the industry works.

Parrot Rubio keeps running the same two parrot lines like a malfunctioning animatronic in a collapsing theme park:

“Iran must never get a nuke.”

“Iran has no military, no leadership, no capability.”

These two talking points are supposed to coexist, but they contradict each other so violently they could power a small city.
Because if Iran truly has no military, no leadership, no capability, no structure, no competence — then why is the Strait of Hormuz behaving like a geopolitical clogged artery?

If Iran is the international equivalent of a broken lawn chair, then why are drones and fast boats still buzzing around like budget‑brand Bond villains? Why are shipping insurers acting like they’re underwriting a demolition derby? Why is global energy distribution wobbling every time a radar blip appears?

Either Iran is a threat, or it isn’t — but Parrot Rubio keeps insisting both at the same time.

And that’s where the February‑to‑now entanglement becomes a dystopian economic farce.

From the moment this conflict kicked off, the narrative has been a surreal loop:

Iran is too weak to matter.

Iran is too dangerous to ignore.

Iran has no functioning military.

Iran is disrupting global shipping.

Iran has no leadership.

Iran is coordinating persistent attacks.

Iran must never get a nuke.

Iran is incapable of anything.

It’s a geopolitical Schrödinger’s cat: simultaneously harmless and destabilizing, depending on which talking point needs to be deployed that day.

Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz — the world’s energy aorta — keeps spasming like it’s possessed.
Tankers reroute.
Insurance spikes.
Oil markets twitch like they’re hooked up to a car battery.
Analysts panic.
And the public is told to “trust the narrative.”

The absurdity is almost elegant.

Because if Iran has no military, then the world’s most powerful military should have neutralized the “non‑military” threat before breakfast.
If Iran has no leadership, then who is coordinating the disruptions?
If Iran has no capability, then why is the global energy supply chain acting like it’s being haunted?

And that’s where the public starts asking the forbidden questions — not because they have proof, but because the contradictions are loud enough to echo:

If Iran is so weak, why hasn’t the threat been eliminated?

If Iran is so strong, why does Parrot Rubio keep insisting they’re not?

If the Strait of Hormuz is “secure,” why does it keep behaving like it’s not?

And who benefits from a global oil market that stays permanently jittery?

Some people — again, without evidence — speculate about intelligence agencies playing both sides of the chessboard to maintain the perfect level of instability.
Not because they know, but because the narrative gaps are big enough to sail a tanker through.

And through it all, Parrot Rubio keeps pressing the nuclear button like a man who was trained to deliver exactly two lines, no matter what the plot is doing around him.
The world has moved on to drones, boats, shipping lanes, and oil flows — but he’s still reciting the same mushroom‑cloud monologue from February.

The result is a geopolitical production that feels like Orwell wrote the script, Idiocracy directed it, and the global oil market financed it:

The official story says Iran has no military.

The real world shows shipping lanes in distress.

And the public is expected to pretend these two things don’t contradict each other.

It’s brutal.
It’s absurd.
It’s economically convenient.
And it’s dystopian enough to make the audience wonder whether the chaos is the crisis — or the business model.

I wish we could get one EV with German technology, Japanese reliability, Lexus comfort, and Chinese value.

The Germans build cars like flagship smartphones: amazing screens, clever software, beautiful features, and then after 5 years you either hand it back and own nothing, or keep it long enough for a sensor, module, screen, subscription, battery valve, or electronic gremlin to ask for your kidneys as a deposit.

Of course, buying is not automatically sainthood either. If you buy an €80k car and lose €50k in depreciation, that money has also gone to car heaven.

Leasing makes sense only if you accept the truth: you are basically mortgaging a car for a long period of time, except at the end you usually do not even own the house on wheels.

So the only honest question is whether the lease cost is better than depreciation, repair risk, warranty comfort, and the money you could have invested elsewhere.

After 4 or 5 years, you return it, own nothing, and are welcomed back to the pedestrian lane with lighter pockets and deeper wisdom.

Meanwhile the dealer smiles like a dentist before surgery.

Retrofitting a coal plant as a nuclear plant strikes me as being a fool's errand. In my 40 years as an engineer in the power industry, I worked in both types of plant. Such a replacement would require a complete gutting of the power train: coal crushers and feeders, burners, boilers, turbine, generator, condenser, deaerator, pipes, valves, instruments, wires - basically anything that's not concrete or steel structure. One would have to build a large special containment vault to contain the reactor and primary steam generator (nuclear-generated steam has more vapor content than coal-generated). One would have to build infrastructure for storing spent fuel. One would have to redesign the control room, retrain or hire all-new nuclear qualified operating, maintenance and engineering staff. All changes would have to be approved by the nuclear regulator. All this assumes you could pass the initial environmental assessment.
It's too bad, because although nuclear plants are expensive to build, they are cheap to operate, because a year's supply of nuclear fuel (which could fill a suburban home) costs a minute fraction of a year's supply of coal (which would fill the Freedom Tower in New York a couple of times).

I used to work with a company they contracted to come do some crane work so they could access something (if I remember correctly they were turbines of some sort, but I’m not 100% certain). I did a little research on this and the primary reason companies don't do this is because the coal being burned in most plants has just enough radioactive material mixed in that gets released during the process that the radioactivity levels are already higher than the maximum level nuclear power plants have to stay under. So while you could gut the coal side of a turbine plant and just drop a reactor in its place you then have to find a way to lower the local radiation levels to below the max limit before you would be granted an operation certificate, which could take years of the building being essentially useless.

Most coal units that are retiring are doing so at the end of a major replacement cycle, meaning they would have a lot of replacements in the boiler, turbines, etc. to keep going. It’s doable, but would not be as cheap or easy as one thinks.

Great video, I'm looking for a new car myself and I never thought in my lifetime I would consider an EV but given the recent concerns of Australia's resource reliance. My mindset has changed and the competition from China are excellent given the prices, when compared to Europe imports.

It took me over 18 months to decide, and a spreadsheet 😁, but in December 2024 I bought a Hyundai Ioniq 5 and haven't looked back. And especially now! I don't have solar panels but with my (Western Australian) energy supplier I get a super off-peak rate between 9am and 3 pm (effectively using other people's excess solar). Before the latest fuel price rises I was paying just over 10% of my previous annual energy costs to run my car. Now, it would be something like 5%! It is sovereign and economic imperative to go electric. Australia spends over $60 billion annually to import petrol and diesel for road transport. This expenditure covers imported oil products, which account for roughly 91% of Australia's fuel consumption. Despite the high cost, government incentives, including fuel tax credits, partially offset the expense of importing this fuel. 
    Import Cost: Australians pay over $60 billion annually for imported petroleum products used in vehicles.
    Fuel Dependency: Road transport accounts for over half (54%) of Australia's total liquid fuel consumption, and the country is 91% reliant on imported fuel.
    Subsidies: Federal and state governments provide billions in support for fossil fuel use, with some analyses suggesting over 16 billion in annual subsidies.
    Net Cost: While generating significant fuel tax revenue, the economic cost of importing this fuel remains a significant burden. 

These figures are sourced from analysis of government data and studies on transport energy, often highlighting the economic impact of imported fuel and the potential for transition to electric vehicles.

The real petroleum fuel crisis hasn’t started yet. If the Strait of Hormuz remains closed much longer, things will get really bad, really fast.

So far, the world has drawn down on afloat and onshore storages that aren’t being freshly pumped out of the Middle East and shipped from the Persian Gulf. These include the 400 million barrels of strategic reserves, as well as the release of Russian and even Iranian oil from sanctions. The world is using up those buffers, and if the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed for a few weeks more, then the world will be forced to begin to make do with far less crude oil/petroleum fuels.

For perspective, global crude oil + lease condensate production in Dec 2025 was 86,058 thousand barrels per day average, per US DoE EIA. Of the ~86 Mb/d of crude oil + condensate produced globally, roughly 40 Mb/d was available for trade in international markets, while the remainder is used internally by oil producer countries.

But ~11-13.8 Mb/d has now evaporated from the ~40 Mb/d crude oil that was traded in international markets pre-conflict. So those countries that were relying on that crude oil, or the petroleum products refined from those feedstocks, like Australia, will now have substantially much less access to supplies of these petroleum products. Rising fuel prices are now inevitable to destroy sufficient global demand.

Oil prices are surging. Inflation is crushing households. And a full-scale Iran war could trigger the biggest economic collapse the modern world has ever seen.
